---
title: "About"
---
*WEBSITE STILL WIP*


Hi there! I’m Edward, an undergrad in the class of 2024 and an Egleston Scholar at Columbia Engineering.

As of now, my main interests lie amidst the fields of Machine Learning and Computational Neuroscience. My ultimate lifetime goal is to contribute to the ongoing pursuit of comprehending what exactly governs consciousness and sentience, which will *hopefully* happen some day (and also *hopefully* not create something along the lines of Ultron or Skynet).

Currently, most of my time is going into two research projects (technically, one research and the other a project) with with Dr. Nakul Verma at Columbia's Computer Science department.

One is on unsupervised sentiment analysis with articles written by people, and the motivation behind the project is to explore new ways and algorithms for unsupervised learning. The main focus is to start with a baseline model and use a form of bootstrapping to improve accuracy. We're also looking into PU Learning methods and seeing if combining NLP and PU Learning works. 

The second one is focused on theoretical machine learning, specifically representation learning and word embeddings. An interesting phenomenon that occurs in the skip-gram model (model for learning vector representations of words) is that they express additive compositionality, which means that although we don't specifically train the skip-gram model to learn the relationships between words of, say, "man + royal = king," the model surprisingly possesses that characteristic. While there has been a lot of work on proving how this can be, the described proofs have unrealistic and strict assumptions and there still isn't a good explanation as to what is going on (AFAIK). We look to investigate this phenomenon and hopefully add to the preceding work on explaining what exactly is happening.

I'm also collaborating with a friend who goes to Cambridge University on a Computer Vision-related project. The general idea is to develop a miniature system that can accurately calculate object distance.

Besides research, I'm also in Columbia Space Initiative (CSI), Columbia's space-themed club. I'm in the RASC-AL team that looks to participate in RASC-AL, a student competition to design deep space mission concepts. Specifically, I'm in charge of designing the propulsion systems (more or less just reading past papers on the topic and coming up with new designs based on previously successful missions).

While I was in Columbia's Formula Racing Club (FSAE), I was working on tire simulation; specifically, applying curve-fitting and coefficient optimization on Pacejka's Magic Formula.

In my free time, I like to program—mainly simulations that I find interesting to see what would happen and reinforcement learning agents to train on the games I make. I also like making robots and tinkering (and Columbia has the perfect space for that—the Makerspace).

If you're interested, here are a few miscellaneous links (most are in Japanese) where I appear:

- [The Egleston Scholars: Weida (Edward) Li](https://www.engineering.columbia.edu/egleston-scholars/weida-edward-li)
- [江副記念財団学術部門第四十九期生：李 為達](https://www.recruit-foundation.org/academic/li/)
- [Containerインタビュー：李 為達](https://container-web.jp/people/15564.html)
- [現役高校生チーム、アメリカのロボット大会に臨む！](https://newswitch.jp/p/10882)

Feel free to reach out to me for anything!

- Email: [wl2787@columbia.edu](mailto:wl2787@columbia.edu)
- GitHub: [NarutatsuRi](https://github.com/narutatsuri)
